sdd-fleet
  • Brief

01

The workspace

A change in a bank usually spans several services, and the risk concentrates at the interfaces between them. The workspace is where that change is planned and driven: a parent folder with the member repos as git submodules and a single Obsidian vault over them — an orchestration layer, not a monorepo. Two ideas make the interfaces safe: the contract is the shared spec, and a computed catalog gives every change its neighbourhood.

Every consequence lives in code; the model judges only the irreducible residue; a human owns the blast radius. Harness Model Human the one rule →

Because the member repos are git submodules pinning exact SHAs, the workspace at a commit is a reproducible snapshot of the whole estate. A hook refuses to spec any story whose epic is not yet ratified — so the ratified contract design is the context every repo inherits.

The contract is the shared spec

The schema — OpenAPI, Avro, proto — is the cross-service spec, and consumer-driven contract tests are its acceptance. A producer's change must not break a consumer's registered expectation; that test is the gate. Tests-first, extended across repos.

Structural awareness is computed, not curated

Each repo carries one small, gated descriptor — identity, consume edges, lifecycle, data classes. The system map falls out of every descriptor and published contract: you maintain per-service metadata; the graph regenerates. The map is consumed from the catalog (Backstage or the minimal equivalent), never maintained as a separate awareness store.

// service.json — repo root · committed · gated · human-owned · JSON (jq-native)
{
  "id": "payments-authoriser",               // stable, never reused
  "team": "payments-core",
  "lifecycle": "production",                 // experimental | production | deprecated
  "data_classes": ["pii", "money_movement"],  // feeds blast-radius
  "produces": ["payments.authorise@3"],       // contract@major — full semver + the spec artifact live in the registry
  "consumes": ["ledger.post@2", "fraud.score@1"]  // these edges build the graph
}

The catalog feeds the gates, not just the prompt. The semver check and the pinned-consumer lookup are both code, leaving the model exactly one call — is this diff breaking beyond its version bump?

The conductor — autonomous dispatch optional

The dispatch above is a pull: a developer runs next-story and starts the ready story. The conductor is the optional autonomous variant of that same resolver — a level-triggered reconciler (it re-reads live state every cycle instead of trusting memory; scripts only, no command, no model) that drives the ready frontier — the set of stories whose dependencies have all published — hands-off across a large estate. Each tick reads ground truth, status plus the registry, fresh; signals the ready set; and holds no private state, so a crash mid-tick is invisible: on restart it resumes from real state. It never invokes the per-repo machine — it only signals — so pull and autonomous dispatch share the exact same ready-frontier core; the only difference is who pulls the trigger. See ADR-0001 for the execution & deployment model.

One writer per unit of work. One orchestrator per repo worktree, one conductor per epic — so two runs never race on the same state as the estate scales.

sdd-fleet — a Claude Code plugin · engineered for audit and replay
Deterministic harness · probabilistic generation · human accountability
